Schatzki's Rings: Recognition, Significance, and Management
  • Source: Clinical Gastroenterology and Hepatology 2026
  • Topic: Lower Esophageal Mucosal Ring - Intermittent Solid Food Dysphagia
  • Author(s): Francesca Forattini, Rena Yadlapati
Historical Evolution
  • First description: Templeton's 1944 monograph (incidental finding)
  • 1953: Schatzki and Gary correlated thin diaphragmatic narrowing above GEJ with intermittent dysphagia
  • 1953: Ingelfinger and Kramer described "contractile ring" visible only during peristalsis
  • Schatzki later established "Schatzki rule": rings <13 mm --> usually symptomatic; >20 mm --> rarely so
  • This threshold led to development of 12.5–13 mm barium tablet for esophagography
  • Histopathologic studies localized ring to squamocolumnar junction
  • Classification distinguishes:
  • A ring: contractile ring, myogenic phenomenon, muscular
  • B ring: mucosal "B ring" composed of squamous epithelium proximally and columnar epithelium distally, mechanical lesion
Epidemiology
  • Prevalence: 6%–14% in routine barium studies (asymptomatic individuals)
  • More common in middle-aged and older adults
  • Sex distribution varies; some cohorts show male predominance
  • Rare in pediatric and young adult populations
  • When present in younger patients: often associated with:
  • Hiatal hernia
  • GERD
  • Eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE)
  • Suggests secondary or adaptive process in younger populations
Clinical Presentation
  • Only 15%–30% of individuals with Schatzki's ring develop symptoms
  • Symptomatic patients present with:
  • Intermittent, nonprogressive dysphagia to solid foods
  • Often of sudden onset and short duration
  • Symptoms localize to lower retrosternal region
  • Weight loss uncommon
  • Eating habits, bolus size, and food consistency influence symptom occurrence
  • Acute food bolus impaction may be initial presentation
  • Among emergent endoscopy for esophageal food impaction: Schatzki's ring represents ~15%–20% of benign structural causes
  • Symptom severity correlates poorly with ring diameter
  • Dysphagia influenced by coexisting:
  • GERD
  • Hiatal hernia
  • Esophageal motility
  • Inflammatory conditions
Pathogenesis Hypotheses
Congenital Theory
  • Proposed as congenital mucosal fold
  • Supported by: smooth symmetry, consistent location, autopsy findings
  • May represent exaggerated anatomic variant of distal esophageal mucosal infolding
  • Counter-evidence: rarity in childhood, frequent association with hernia/GERD in adults
Plication Theory
  • Longitudinal shortening of distal esophagus --> circumferential mucosal infolding at GEJ
  • Often in setting of sliding hiatal hernia
  • Strong association between Schatzki's ring and hiatal hernia supports this mechanism
  • Counter-evidence: not all patients with hernia develop rings
Inflammatory Theory (Most Widely Accepted)
  • Chronic inflammation and healing at squamocolumnar junction
  • Particularly from GERD
  • Abnormal acid exposure demonstrated in many symptomatic patients
  • Long-term PPI therapy reduces recurrence after dilation
  • Barrett's esophagus reported less frequently in patients with Schatzki's ring
  • Hypothesis: ring may be protective against proximal acid exposure (unproven)
  • Eosinophil-mediated inflammation and coexistence with EoE reported in younger patients
  • Current adult data do not support clear association between EoE and Schatzki's ring
Diagnostic Evaluation
Radiologic Diagnosis (Barium Esophagography)
  • Most sensitive diagnostic modality for Schatzki's ring
  • Findings: thin, smooth, circumferential narrowing at GEJ
  • Often just proximal to small hiatal hernia
  • Prone full-column techniques with adequate distension: 95% sensitivity
  • Provides valuable information on:
  • Motility
  • Associated reflux
Endoscopic Diagnosis (Upper GI Endoscopy)
  • Allows exclusion of alternative diagnoses
  • Enables histologic sampling and therapeutic intervention
  • Appearance: thin mucosal diaphragm at or just above squamocolumnar junction
  • Must distinguish from peptic strictures (which are longer, irregular, associated with chronic reflux-related mucosal injury)
  • Overall sensitivity: ~58%
  • Sensitivity varies by ring diameter:
  • Rings ≤13 mm: 82% sensitivity
  • Rings 20–25 mm: 25% sensitivity
  • Up to 40% of radiographically visible rings missed during routine endoscopy
  • Reasons for miss:
  • Reduction of hiatal hernia conceals distal ring
  • Excessive insufflation flattens subtle lesion
  • Detection tips: careful withdrawal of scope from stomach into distal esophagus with adequate luminal distension
  • Adjunctive maneuvers: retroflexion or abdominal compression may improve detection
Esophageal Manometry
  • No role in diagnosing Schatzki's ring itself (ring lacks intrinsic motor activity)
  • Useful in patients with persistent or atypical symptoms
  • Identifies coexisting motility disorders contributing to dysphagia
Treatment and Prognosis
General Principles
  • Directed toward:
  • Symptom relief
  • Prevention of recurrence
  • Treatment of associated conditions (GERD, mucosal inflammation)
  • Indicated only in symptomatic patients (benign nature, relative stability over time)
  • Patient reassurance: nonmalignant condition
  • Behavioral measures:
  • Careful chewing
  • Slow ingestion of solid foods
  • Overall prognosis excellent
  • Dysphagia recurrence common --> necessitates stepwise management approach
Endoscopic Dilation (First-Line Therapy)
  • Cornerstone of therapy
  • Objective: controlled radial expansion until mucosal disruption occurs
  • Restores adequate luminal diameter
  • Effective with both:
  • Bougie techniques
  • Balloon dilation techniques
  • No clear superiority of either when performed by experienced operators
  • Immediate symptom relief achieved in most patients
  • Recurrence common:
  • ~1/3 within first year
  • Up to 1/2 over longer follow-up
  • Often requires repeat dilation
  • Ring thickness (not luminal diameter alone) associated with need for repeat dilation
  • Long-term acid suppression significantly:
  • Prolongs symptom-free intervals
  • Reduces likelihood of ring reformation
  • Maintenance PPI therapy following dilation is standard practice
  • Acid suppression alone may improve symptoms and ring caliber in selected patients
  • Repeat dilation is safe and effective when symptoms recur
  • Perforation risk consistently <1%
Endoscopic Incisional Therapy (Alternative)
  • For recurrent or refractory Schatzki's ring
  • Unlike dilation (stretches), incisional techniques mechanically divide fibrotic mucosa
  • Techniques:
  • Biopsy-forceps disruption
  • Electrosurgical incision
  • Needle-knife incision
  • Prospective randomized study showed:
  • Electrosurgical incision comparable symptom relief to bougie dilation
  • Longer symptom-free interval: 7.99 vs 5.86 months
  • Low complication rates
Surgical Approaches
  • Largely historical and anecdotal interest today
  • Considered only in exceptional circumstances:
  • Large or complicated hiatal hernia requiring operative correction
  • Multiple endoscopic attempts fail to achieve durable relief
  • Objective: restore normal gastroesophageal anatomy (not excise ring itself)
Key Take-Home Points
  • Schatzki's ring is common, benign cause of intermittent solid-food dysphagia
  • Often underrecognized on upper endoscopy
  • Likely reflects interplay of:
  • Mechanical factors (hiatal hernia, plication)
  • Inflammatory factors (GERD, chronic inflammation)
  • Not a single disease entity
  • Endoscopic dilation + long-term acid suppression provides effective symptom relief in most patients
  • Recurrence is frequent but repeat dilation safe and effective
